title: Cell division — how sex cells are made (meiosis)
examTip: Meiosis comes up most often in comparison with mitosis — know the differences cold. The key numbers are 2 divisions, 4 daughter cells, 23 chromosomes each. Genetic variation through crossing over is also a common exam point.
introTips: You already know that mitosis produces identical copies. Meiosis does something completely different — it produces cells that are all genetically unique.
You'll see why halving the chromosome number is essential for fertilisation to work, and why the two divisions of meiosis are different from each other.
The mitosis vs meiosis comparison table is one of the most commonly examined topics in this whole unit — know every row of it.
intro: Every human alive is the result of two cells fusing — a sperm and an egg. If those cells were produced by normal cell division (mitosis), they'd each carry 46 chromosomes, and fertilisation would produce a cell with 92. Next generation, 184. It would spiral out of control within a few generations. Evolution solved this with meiosis: a special type of cell division that halves the chromosome number to 23, so that when sperm meets egg, the total comes back to 46. And as a bonus, it generates more genetic variation than almost any other process in biology.

keyPoints.heading: Why meiosis exists — the halving problem
keyPoints.content: The whole point of meiosis is to produce gametes — sex cells (sperm and eggs) — that carry half the normal chromosome number. A normal human cell has 46 chromosomes (23 pairs). Meiosis reduces this to 23 — one from each pair. A cell with half the chromosome number is called haploid. When a haploid sperm (23) fertilises a haploid egg (23), the resulting cell — called a zygote — has 46 chromosomes again. The diploid number is restored. Without this halving step, every generation would double the chromosome count and the organism would not survive.
writeBullets: Meiosis produces gametes (sperm and eggs) with half the chromosome number
Normal body cells = diploid (46 chromosomes, 23 pairs)
Gametes = haploid (23 chromosomes — one from each pair)
Fertilisation: haploid sperm (23) + haploid egg (23) = diploid zygote (46)
This maintains the correct chromosome number across generations

keyTerms.def: Cell division that produces four genetically different haploid cells (gametes) — 23 chromosomes each
A sex cell — sperm or egg — produced by meiosis; contains 23 chromosomes (haploid)
A cell containing one set of chromosomes — 23 in humans
The fertilised egg cell, formed when sperm and egg fuse — diploid (46 chromosomes)

keyPoints.memTip: Haploid = HALF. Meiosis → gametes → 23. Mitosis → body cells → 46. Write this as a simple equation: sperm (23) + egg (23) = zygote (46). Cover it and redraw it from memory.

keyPoints.heading: What actually happens — two divisions
keyPoints.content: Unlike mitosis, meiosis involves two separate division steps. In the first division, the cell separates the 23 homologous chromosome pairs — so each of the two new cells gets one chromosome from each pair, giving 23 chromosomes (but each is still made of two joined copies, called chromatids). In the second division, those joined chromatids are separated, like mitosis. The final result: four haploid daughter cells, each containing 23 chromosomes. Critically, because of shuffling during the first division (more on that below), all four cells are genetically different from each other and from the parent cell.
writeBullets: Meiosis has TWO divisions (unlike mitosis which has one)
First division: separates homologous pairs → 2 cells, each with 23 chromosomes (as chromatid pairs)
Second division: separates chromatids → 4 cells, each with 23 single chromosomes
Final result: 4 haploid daughter cells, all genetically DIFFERENT from each other

keyTerms.def: The matching pair of chromosomes — one from mum, one from dad — that carry genes for the same characteristics
One of the two identical strands that make up a duplicated chromosome, joined at the centromere

keyPoints.memTip: Two divisions, four cells. Draw it: one cell → two cells (first division) → four cells (second division). Label the chromosome number at each stage: 46 → 23 → 23. Then write it as a flow without looking.

keyPoints.heading: Mitosis vs meiosis — know every difference
keyPoints.content: AQA frequently asks you to compare mitosis and meiosis in a table. Here are the four key differences. Number of divisions: mitosis = one, meiosis = two. Daughter cells produced: mitosis = 2, meiosis = 4. Chromosome number in daughter cells: mitosis = 46 (diploid), meiosis = 23 (haploid). Genetic variation: mitosis produces genetically identical cells; meiosis produces genetically different cells. Also: mitosis is used for growth, repair and replacement; meiosis is used only to produce gametes. Location: mitosis happens in all body cells; meiosis only happens in the testes and ovaries.
writeBullets: Mitosis: 2 daughter cells | Meiosis: 4 daughter cells
Mitosis: genetically identical | Meiosis: genetically different
Mitosis: growth, repair, replacement | Meiosis: gamete production only
Mitosis: all body cells | Meiosis: testes and ovaries only

keyTerms.def: A cell with the full set of chromosomes — 46 in humans (two copies of each)
A cell with half the chromosome number — 23 in humans (one copy of each)

keyPoints.memTip: Cover the table below and recite the four differences out loud: divisions (1 vs 2), cells produced (2 vs 4), chromosome number (46 vs 23), variation (identical vs different). Do it twice.

keyPoints.heading: Genetic variation — why meiosis mixes the pack
keyPoints.content: Meiosis doesn't just halve the chromosome number — it also generates enormous genetic variation. This happens in two ways. First, crossing over: during the first division, homologous chromosomes pair up and swap segments of DNA with each other. This mixes alleles between chromosomes so that each new chromosome is a blend of the originals. Second, independent assortment: when the 23 pairs line up and separate, which chromosome from each pair goes to which new cell is completely random. The combination of crossing over and independent assortment means that no two gametes are ever genetically identical (unless they're from identical twins sharing the same zygote). This is why you and your siblings share parents but are not copies of each other.
writeBullets: Crossing over: homologous chromosomes swap segments of DNA during the first division
Independent assortment: which chromosome from each pair goes to which cell is random
Both processes create gametes that are all genetically unique
Result: offspring from the same parents are genetically different from each other

keyTerms.def: The exchange of DNA segments between homologous chromosomes during meiosis — mixes alleles
The random separation of homologous chromosome pairs during meiosis — each gamete gets a different random combination
A version of a gene — for example, one allele for brown eyes, another for blue

keyPoints.memTip: Two sources of variation in meiosis: Crossing Over (CO) and Independent Assortment (IA). Remember: CI — Crossing over shuffles within chromosomes; Independent assortment shuffles which chromosome goes where. Think: 'Meiosis deals a new hand of cards every time.'

1.question: How many chromosomes does a human gamete contain, and what term describes this?
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right — gametes are haploid, carrying 23 chromosomes. When two gametes fuse at fertilisation the diploid number of 46 is restored.
1.wrongFeedback: Gametes have 23 chromosomes — half the normal number. This is described as haploid. Body cells (and the zygote formed at fertilisation) have 46 chromosomes and are described as diploid.

3.question: Which row in the mitosis vs meiosis comparison is WRONG?
options: Mitosis: 2 daughter cells | Meiosis: 4 daughter cells
Mitosis: genetically identical | Meiosis: genetically identical
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Well spotted — meiosis produces genetically DIFFERENT cells, not identical ones. The genetic variation from crossing over and independent assortment is the whole point.
3.wrongFeedback: The wrong row is the one saying both produce genetically identical cells. Mitosis does produce identical cells — but meiosis produces genetically DIFFERENT cells. That's one of the most important differences between the two processes.

commonMistakes: Saying meiosis produces 2 daughter cells — it produces 4. Mitosis produces 2. This single number is one of the most commonly confused facts on AQA papers.
Getting the chromosome numbers backwards — meiosis produces haploid cells (23 chromosomes), not diploid. Mitosis produces diploid cells (46 chromosomes).
Saying meiosis happens in all body cells — meiosis only happens in the testes (sperm) and ovaries (eggs). Everywhere else in the body, it's mitosis.
Forgetting that meiosis produces genetically DIFFERENT cells — if you write 'genetically identical' for meiosis you'll lose the mark. The whole point of meiosis is variation.
Describing meiosis as having one division like mitosis — meiosis has TWO divisions. The first separates homologous pairs; the second separates chromatids. Make sure you state this clearly.
examTips: If a question asks you to compare mitosis and meiosis, structure your answer as a table or paired points — examiners mark by comparison, so 'mitosis produces 2 cells whereas meiosis produces 4' scores more reliably than listing each separately.
For questions about genetic variation: you need to name at least one mechanism — either crossing over or independent assortment. 'Random mixing of chromosomes' on its own is too vague for full marks.
When a question mentions gametes, fertilisation, or sexual reproduction — that's your cue to write about meiosis. When it mentions growth, repair or replacement — that's mitosis.

videoLinks.label: Meiosis and Genetic Variation

revisionCardBullets: Meiosis = cell division that produces 4 genetically DIFFERENT haploid daughter cells (23 chromosomes each)
Purpose: to make gametes (sperm and eggs) so fertilisation restores the diploid number (46)
Haploid = 23 chromosomes (gametes) | Diploid = 46 chromosomes (body cells and zygote)
Meiosis has TWO divisions: first separates homologous pairs (46→23); second separates chromatids (4 cells)
Mitosis: 1 division, 2 identical cells, 46 chromosomes | Meiosis: 2 divisions, 4 different cells, 23 chromosomes
Mitosis = growth, repair, replacement | Meiosis = gamete production (testes + ovaries only)
Crossing over: homologous chromosomes swap DNA segments during meiosis → new allele combinations
Independent assortment: random sorting of chromosomes into new cells → unique combinations every time
Both crossing over and independent assortment explain why siblings share parents but aren't identical copies
